title: MAC Addresses
description: 48-bit hardware addresses burned into every NIC — used for local network frame delivery.
fullExplanation: A MAC (Media Access Control) address is a 48-bit identifier assigned to every network interface. Written as 6 hex pairs (e.g. A4:C3:F0:12:34:56). The first 3 bytes (OUI — Organizationally Unique Identifier) identify the manufacturer. The last 3 bytes are the device-specific serial. MAC addresses are used for local delivery within a subnet — routers use IP addresses for inter-network routing. Switches build a CAM table mapping MAC addresses to ports.

structure.name: OUI
structure.description: Organizationally Unique Identifier — assigned by IEEE to manufacturer

specialAddresses.description: Delivered to all devices on segment (ARP requests, DHCP discover)

keyFacts: OUI lookup: identify manufacturer from first 3 bytes (Wireshark does this automatically)
MAC addresses can be spoofed in software — not a reliable security mechanism
CAM table: switch maps MAC → port for unicast forwarding
Unknown unicast: switch floods frame to all ports (like a hub) until MAC learned
MAC aging: CAM entries expire after ~300 seconds of inactivity
Randomized MAC: iOS/Android randomize MAC per network for privacy
